sentences.text: There was the-- you couldn't go in, in every store.
We couldn't mix up with other children.
And they-- they had laws against us, different laws that--
Curfews, what we were allowed to do, what we were not allowed to do.
But I remember at night, we never went out.
And we had to go only with the star of David.
We couldn't go otherwise.
We couldn't go in to other stores what was not for Jewish people specially.
But right-- right-- a short time after we were in Vienna, my father-- they loved him at work.
And they-- one day they told him, Samuel, Herr Hacker, Mr.
Hacker, we cannot keep you anymore because you are Jewish.
He had to-- they didn't let him work there.
Luckily-- first of all, I want to tell you about my mother, that she was the-- the mother for the whole family, for everybody.
All the children from my uncles-- from my mother's side and from father's side-- they always came to our house.
Everybody loved my mother because she had always time for everybody.
My father and my mother-- Leo and me, the children, they were all their life.
They tried-- they didn't eat to give us, to keep us healthy.
And they did everything.
When my father was-- yeah-- right after my father was fired from his job because he was Jewish, suddenly they started transporting people.
And one of the first transport, they were saying-- because my mother's brother, Moritz, had probably eight children.
And the other brother, Iula, the shoemaker, he had 10 or 12 children.
They were sent-- I think even in 1940 already-- they were sent to Poland, the whole families.
Huh?
They didn't tell us.
I found out later.
But they were saying-- they told him they will send him to a different country-- different place for work.
So slowly the family became--
Smaller.
And my uncle went.
The two uncles with all the children went.
It happens that I had another sister of my father in Vienna, that she had two children.
And one of her sons, his name was Otto Bock, he worked for the Jewish Community.
Then, when they started sending the transport, they gave orders-- Eichmann gave orders to the Jewish community.
Every family that leaves, they had to go there, take all their belongings, the furniture, whatever was left in the apartment because people could leave only with their suitcase.
If my uncle had 10 children, they let him go with four, five suitcases.
The rest, whatever was-- the Jewish community had the responsibility to collect everything what was in these places, if it's pictures or furniture or whatever was there.
And they had to bring it to the order where-- Mr.
Eichmann gave the orders then, I think-- to the places where they wanted it.
So my cousin, Otto, he worked in the Jewish community.
And when my father was laid off in the--
--in the bakery, he could arrange for him to work in the Jewish community.
Luckily, because this kept us a lot longer than everybody else-- and our family in Vienna.
After the two uncles left from my mother's side, I had another Uncle Willie, that Walter, Kurt and Erich, that they visited us very often.
I had another uncle, Uncle Philip, that his daughter was married.
And they took her husband and sent him to Poland.
We didn't know then it's Poland.
But they sent him away.
When she heard that-- her name was Greta-- she committed suicide with her child.
She went in the kitchen, opened the gas, and killed herself.
That was another uncle.
Months, maybe three, four months after my uncle-- the two uncles went to Poland, suddenly one of their sons and sisters, Walter Riegler and Anne Riegler, came back from Poland.
And they reported to us that when they arrived everybody in Poland, they put all-- thousands of families in one little place, without food, without nothing.
And they started to-- these people had to start digging graves.
And they killed them.
So my cousin and his sister ran away.
And they arrived in Vienna.
And they told her that.
And they were hiding in our place, in our apartment, too.
How did you feel about it as a child?
Were you scared?
I was scared-- the moment00 from the moment that Hitler came, I was already scared.
I was really scared because I-- I lost all my friends.
All the friends that I thought they're my friends, they start calling me dirty Jew, schwein, pig Jew.
And nobody wanted to talk to us anymore.
The only one that we had was our family.
And they were afraid, too.
What I didn't mention before, my uncle, Max, that came with us to Vienna, too, and his aunt-- and my aunt and her son, they arrested him and sent him to Dachau, the concentration camp.
But in that time, in 1940, they started building this concentration camp, Dachau.
And after he was there five or six months, they told him that if he is leaving Austria in 24 hours with his family, they-- they release him from the concentration camp.
That time, they didn't kill.
In the camps, they build it only.
And one day really he came back.
His son, Fritz Mordechai, left before he came back.
He got a certificate to go to Palestine.
When he came back from Dachau, I remember, he packed with his wife and left Austria.
I later on found out that he went with the-- with the ship, to Israel with the Holzer family.
If you remember, I told you in our village was our second Jewish family, Holzer, that they had two children.
So he, with his wife, the Holzer with their two children, went to Israel, probably in 1940, illegal.
They came to Haifa.
The story I tell you I was told later.
They came to Haifa.
And the British captured the ship.

So when they came to the port Haifa, the Haganah-- the-- then it was the illegal Jewish army from Palestine-- they put a mine in the boat, and with the mine-- that it will take four or five hours until the ship will sink.
And all the people will come down.
But it was too big.
The ship went down in an hour, hour and a half.
My uncle and his brother-in-law saved the two children.
And my aunt, his wife and her sister, the Holzer, they both went down in 1940. 400 people, then, drowned-- went down with the ship.
And there is a memorial in Haifa for all 400 people there.
There are-- they have there their graves, one big grave.
So after my uncle left, we were still in Vienna, the family together.
It-- it was a life that you didn't have no friends.
Except family, you didn't have nobody.
Nobody knew us.
It's so hard to understand this-- how could it be that all the people are against us, that we couldn't find somebody to help us or to be with us or to offer a good word for us?
It was very hard to find somebody.
In our house, we had another family-- that she was Jewish, Mrs.
Stiegler.
And-- and my-- and she had a son, Pauli, that they were half-Jewish.
So my brother found a good friend in this Pauli.
That--and sometimes she invited us-- she had probably more food.
She invited us kids to come to her house to eat.
But I never forget her name.
And later on, I found out that her son is living still today in Vienna.
And he told me stories about my brother that I never knew, that-- what he did with him.
And they were really friends.

It started-- slowly, all our family except my cousin that worked in the Jewish community and my uncle with his sister and my cousins from Walter, that we didn't almost have nobody, because slowly they sent everybody.
The [NON-ENGLISH] that lived in our apartment with their three children were sent away.
Tante Rose--- that her daughter committed suicide and her husband died-- she was sent away.
They were round up.
They came to pick them up-- the SS-- and took them away.
Where?
We didn't know then.
Later on, I found out the [NON-ENGLISH] probably were sent, too, to Poland, where they were killed, or to Auschwitz, I don't know.
The Tante Rose was sent to Theresienstadt, to the ghetto.
Even my cousin's son that worked for the Jewish Community, Otto Bock, his mother and father had to be sent away.
Even he couldn't help them to stay in Vienna.
All the family-- there was nobody actually left except him and my uncle.
They all were sent away, probably the older people were sent to Theresienstadt.
In 1942-- they-- no-- in April of '42, I had my bar mitzvah in Vienna-- that Walter's family came to us, and some other families.
In October 1942, there rounded us up, and they rounded up Walter's family.
As far as I remember, there was a knock on the door.
And they came in, the SS.
And they said, you have to pack your stuff, and you have to be in Bahnhof-- by the train, in so and so much time, two hours or so.
So we had-- the only thing we can do is taking some suitcases.
They told us we're going to a working camp.
We walked to the train station.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If you ask me, I don't remember.
They told us we have to be by the train station, the whole family.
And in the train station, we met Walter, my cousin, and his family.
And they loaded us on a train for us, unknown where we will go.
We were frightening.
We-- we didn't know what will be with us, where we will go, what we will do.
But it was-- it was terrible.
There were thousands of people.
It was a train full of people.
I don't know exactly how many.
But I-- I don't know how long it took.
It took us, probably, five, six days till we went and we came to Theresienstadt.
It was a train-- not-- it was a train that was like wagons that you put in--
Cattle-- or it was a train that you put in material, a closed train.
There was no seats or something.
It was a train that you didn't have a seat where to sit.
We had all to stand or sit on the suitcases.
Boxcars.
But the cars were not open.
It had a roof.
It was-- it had a roof, it had little windows with bars on it.
In this boxcar, I-- I-- if I can remember, it was about 70 or 80 people.
It was loaded.
It was full.
I don't remember how we urinated.
I don't remember what we did for a toilet.
I don't know.
I know only it was terrible.
We didn't have nothing to drink.
We had only food what we took with us.
What I never will forget is we had some jewelry at home, and what we brought to Vienna.
And we had a handbag.
And before we left our home-- our apartment in Vienna-- my mother put everything in a cooking top and put over it-- she first made [NON-ENGLISH] they call it in Austria.
She took [GERMAN]-- flour and oil, and cooked it and put it over that.
And in this handbag, we had all our jewelry.
This we took with us.
This we could-- they didn't-- we couldn't take furniture, but suitcases or handbags we could take.
And whatever food we had, we took.
And we had to eat on the train.
The memories of Vienna-- Vienna-- as a child, I remember it was a beautiful city.
I remember they had a [GERMAN], what they call it here, like a little Disneyland, that you could see.
There was forest.
There was movies.
There was all kind of thing.
There were many things we couldn't go into it.
But Vienna, as I remember, it was a beautiful city.
But we had a terrible life because we had to go with the Star of David and we were recognized wherever we went.
And there were certain things we were allowed to do and certain things not.
When we arrived in Theresienstadt, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we left the train.
And I don't know how many people-- thousands-- but there was no trucks or cars waiting for us.
We had to walk from the train.
Later on, the station was closer to the ghetto.
But when we arrived-- we arrived in October 1942-- we had to walk to the ghetto with SS, and Jewish guards on the side.
There was-- in the ghetto was a Jewish Judenrat, they called it-- the Jewish community, elders-- that they ran the ghetto.
They got orders from the Germans.
But they ran actually the ghetto.
We went through a registration place.
And the ghetto, when we arrived, it looked terrible.
It had big walls.
It was a-- It was, in World War I, it was kazerne.
It was a camp for military, for army.
Brick walls all around.
There was big, brick walls with deep, deep--
Trenches.
And in the ghetto by itself was all kazernes, different military headquarters.
And they separated us right away.
Me, I was separated from my parents.
My brother was with my mother and father.
And the same thing happened to Walter.
They separated-- Walter came with me.
We went in a--
Walter is my cousin that-- from my uncle's son.
And since then, we went in a home of children.
Walter and me, we went in one room.
And it was in a kazerne, but the whole-- the whole kazerne-- the whole building was with children.
In the room, as far as I remember, there were beds three stories high.
And it went from wall to wall.
To go in, you had to go in from the front of the bed.
And on the side, you had probably one or two feet that people could go in from the side.
In the room probably were-- I don't know exactly, but probably 20 to 30 kids.
We had our own [NON-ENGLISH], our own teacher in the ghetto-- in the room.
Later on, they called us [NON-ENGLISH], what's called fire, we were-- all the kids in our room, we were like a group organization.
There were rooms where girls were, younger children.
And in the ghetto, we didn't have-- as far as I remember, we didn't carry anymore the Star of David because they were all inmates, only-- only Jewish inmates.
In the ghetto, I-- I don't remember.
I think they shaved my hair.
My parents and my brother were separated.
They were together.
And they found other aunts from my cousin in Vienna, Tante Reizi Bock, her name, Tante Dele.
Another sister.
Dele-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father's sister, she found there.
Then, in another room, was Uncle Willie with his wife, and Kurt.
And there was another older brother.
And in the-- in the-- in the ghetto, it was times that family didn't have.
But we were studying in the room.
We studied about-- the teacher teached us different courses of religious, Zionism, history, different kinds.
The only thing I remember-- we had to go up early.
And I went to work then, too.
I had to go to work.
I don't remember what time.
I know only one thing, that we were hungry all the time.
We were hungry.
And we looked how to find food.
And in the kazernes, I remember they had in underground, they had potatoes for the kitchens-- for the big kitchens to cook.
And we were kids-- we went always by to look to find-- we made sticks with something to get a potato out.
Or when they were transferring from one place to the other, we tried to steal a potato.
Hungry, I know we were all of the time.
If they would find us, they hit us.
But luckily, we really were never caught.
But we visited always-- yeah, and then, later on, when we were in the ghetto, my father again, they looked for people for work.
I started working in a shoe factory repairing shoes.
How, I don't know.
My father started working in a bakery.
And when he came home, he always put under his shirt, he put a quarter loaf of bread or half a loaf, whatever he could take that they shouldn't catch him.
Well, I remember, too, we had long pants that were closed on the bottom.
And we were stealing potatoes.
We always put them in the-- in the pants-- they went down.
And this way we had some-- and then we went back to the rooms.
We cook them, eat.
In the ghetto, we didn't have-- as far as I remember, we didn't have a uniform.
They had special money there.
I think it was only for a show, because we didn't use the money.
I don't remember using the money.
We could visit our parents.
I saw my mother.
I saw my father.
Not too often, but we visited them.
They were very, very anxious always to see them.
But we couldn't always go.
I worked there.
And suddenly, there were many times that-- one of these days, I remember they-- they said they will send children to Switzerland.
And they collected about-- whoever wants to go.
And there were children that were running to go.
And they looked for teachers that were free-- on their free will to go with them.
There was one Viennese, Arlo Mensch that went with them.
And they made a transport of 1,000 children that lay down-- when I was in Auschwitz, I found out that they were sent straight to the gas chambers.
They started sending transports from Theresienstadt.
They started early in '41-- in '43, they sent transports.
And I had my cousin from Vienna that was running away from Poland with his sister, he ended up in Theresienstadt, too.
And I know whenever he had to go to transport, he always ran away.
They knew that it happened something bad.
They didn't tell us.
But they know that every transport that they had to go, they never went.
They always found a place how to go away.
The Juden elders.
And there was a Judenrat.
The Jewish community, the olderly Jews, they made a list.
They decided who has to go.
And they made a list of 2,000, 3,000.
The Germans probably came to them, telling them, this transport, we want 2,000 to 3,000 people.
And they made the list.
And they let you know that you had to go.
In beginning 1944, there started transports that children from our room already went.
And because I repaired shoes, I had tools from repairing shoes.
And on the first transport, the children that went from our room, I start fixing them the shoes.
I wanted them to have good shoes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What I forgot to mention-- our rooms was not only from Austria.
We had kids from all over the world.
We had children from Holland, Yugoslavia, Germany.
It was from-- community-- children from all over the world.
It had nothing-- all Jewish people, but had nothing to do with Austria.
From Europe.
And we were all together.
We helped each other.
We tried to help and do whatever one can for the other one.
But Walter and me, I think we were the only Austrians in this room.
I fixed the shoes.
Then the first transport, everything was OK.
On the second transport, I repaired the shoes for a guy.
And I always had to warm the iron to soften the wax to fix the shoes.
And I didn't-- the hot wax to heat this metal finished.
And I tried to use liquid.
And I tried to use the liquid to put in a little can.
And this whole thing started a fire.
And I started burning by myself.
I burned by myself.
And the whole room-- the beds with the mattress-- everything starts burning.
And I start screaming and running around like a fire.
My whole body was burned.
Luckily, one of the children took a mattress and put it on me.
He pushed me down and put the mattress on me.
So it stopped the fire.
But I was burned.
And I had third degree--
Fire damage.
And they bandaged me.
And I had to work with crutches.
I couldn't walk like that.
I don't know if it was a hospital or not.
I don't know.
I know only that I got bandages-- if they did it in my block.
But I couldn't walk.
And they gave me crutches.
The same time that it happened to me, the second transport left.
My mother got sick.
And she had an operation.
Probably there was a hospital.
But they operated on her.
I don't know what-- as a child, they told me it's a woman-- a woman thing.
I didn't know what, but they operated on her.
And it was very hard for her to walk.
Sadly, it came the third transport.
And they said, the Hackers are on this transport, too, to leave ghetto Theresienstadt.
This was in 1944, in the month of May.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As I was sick, my mother was sick.
They went to Murmelstein-- he was the Juden Altester.
He was the older--
The Jewish commandant, the head of the ghetto.
And they told him that I'm sick, I'm burned, my mother is sick, we cannot go.
The answer from him was, the Hacker family is too gross-- the Hacker family is too big.
I cannot leave them here.
You have to go.
He figured, my Uncle Willie, with his wife and the three children, and we were four, so we were nine Hackers.
So he couldn't allow us to stay in the ghetto.
We had to go.
So we had to backpack-- we couldn't-- we could take suitcases with us.
They told us we were going to a camp to work.
They didn't tell us concentration camp.
They go-- we'll-- we'll be sent to another place for work-- to work.
I remember we had to walk to the train station.
I walked on crutches.
My mother was hard to walk.
And we all went together.
They came together on one Platz, in a Hauptplatz--
